questions.description: Jill is the manager of a creche. Conditions Of Sale.
questions.text: Jill is the manager of a creche. In January 2023 she sent an email to Supplies Ltd to enquire about purchasing a laminator for the business. In her email she explained that the laminator would need to be "suitable to laminate large posters and artwork foe the creche, up to A2 in size". Supplies Ltd sent her the following reply via email: "Hi Jill, For small businesses we recommend the XL Pro-Laminator. It's perfect for bigger jobs and posters but still easy for your employees to operate. Current price is EUR150, available on our website at the link below........" Jill clicked on the link to the Supplies Ltd website and bought the XL Pro-Laminator directly from the website. Before she entered her payment details, she ticked a box that stated that she "accepts the terms and conditions of sale" but she did not click through the link provided to read these. The laminator arrived in 2 days, but it turns out that it can only laminate objects up to A3 in size, and will not laminate anything as big as the A2 size that Jill needs. Jill is furious, as the creche operates on a tight budget and this laminator does not fulfil their needs. However, when Jill contacts Supplies Ltd to get a refund, they refuse. Supplies Ltd says that there is nothing wrong with the laminator, which works perfectly fine, and they also point to Clause 5 of their terms and conditions which states: "5: Supplies Ltd is not liable for any breach of statutory warranty of merchantability or of fitness for a particular purpose and will not issue refunds." Advise Jill. In your answer, you can assume that Jill is acting in the course of business.

questions.description: Jack is a 70 year old farmer who lives on his own in a rural area in the… Advise Colm.
questions.text: Jack is a 70 year old farmer who lives on his own in a rural area in the west of Ireland. He is in ill health and quite dependent on his neighbour, Sheila, who regularly visits him and assists him with basic household jobs such as cooking and cleaning. Sheila minds Jack's bank account books .or him and occasionally drives him to his local bank. One day when Sheila drives Jack to his local bank Jack transfers €20,000 from his personal savings account to Sheila's bank account. Jack does not discuss the transaction in any detail with any bank representative; nor does he receive any independent legal advice on the transaction. Two months later, Sheila approaches Jack and asks him to sign a petition to stop the closure of a local school, Jack doesn't have his reading glasses and so he signs the petition without reading it. Jack's nephew Colm visits Jack later that year and upon investigation discovers that the "petition" is, in fact, a bank guarantee form which guarantees Sheila's debts up to €25,000. Colm is concerned that his uncle may become liable under this guarantee and wants to know if there is any way the guarantee may be void. He also wants to know if the transfer of €20,000 to Sheila can be set aside. Advise Colm.

questions.description: Sorcha is a world-famous Irish actress. Company.
questions.text: Sorcha is a world-famous Irish actress. In October 2022 she agrees with the film company Irish Movies Ltd to play the lead role in a film called "The Rings of the banshee". She is to receive a payment of €2 million for making the film. Once Sorcha signs the contract, Irish Movies Ltd incurs substantial costs in getting ready for production. In particular, it spends €750,000 on producing a movie set and a further €250,000 on securing special effects experts. However, shortly after signing the contract, Sorcha informs Irish Movies Ltd that she will no longer be appearing in the film, saying that she wants to take her career in a different direction. Irish Movies Ltd decides that without Sorcha there's no point going ahead with the film, and therefore cancels production. They predict that if the. film had gone ahead with Sorcha as planned that they would have made profits of €5 million. They now wish to sue Sorcha for their lost profits. and for their wasted expenditure. However, Sorcha says that Irish Movies Ltd could easily have hired another actress to replace her:, and that the real reason they cancelled production was that the film script for "The Rings of the Banshee" was so terrible that they would not have made any profit from the film had it gone ahead. Advise Sorcha.

questions.description: Todd is the manager of a large construction company, Construction Ltd. Company.
questions.text: Todd is the manager of a large construction company, Construction Ltd. He needs an XL Super Crane for his latest project, so he emails Sarah, the manager of Plant Ltd, to enquire about hiring an XL Super Crane for a 2-year period. Sarah emails the following response to Todd: "Hi Todd, we are happy to offer the XL Super Crane for a 2 year lease, at a rate of €5,090 per month payable in monthly instalments, plus an initial delivery charge of €1,000. This offer is subject to Plant Ltd.'s standard terms and conditions, which are attached to this email." Term 6 of Plant Ltd.'s standard terms and conditions was a price variation clause which provided that the rent could be increased by up to 10% during the course of the hire period, in the event of changes in market conditions. Todd likes the price that is offered, but isn't happy with Term 6 of the standard terms and conditions. He edits the terms and conditions that Sarah has sent him by deleting Term 6. He then replies by email to Sarah, saying "That's great Sarah, I'm happy with the attached terms.Can you deliver next week?" The attached terms and conditions look exactly like the ones Sarah originally sent, but without Term 6. Sarah replies saying "That's perfect, we will organize delivery for next week." She does not notice that the terms and conditions attached to the email are different from the ones she originally sent out and does not refer to this in her email. The XL Super Crane is delivered in January, and invoices are sent in January and February for €5,000 per month. However, in March, Sarah sends an invoice for €5,500, referring to a change in market conditions and Term 6 of the contract as an explanation for the increase in price. Todd is refusing to pay the increased price. He does not deny that ..ere has been a change in market conditions, but he argues that Term 6 is not pat of i. contract and was never agreed to by him. Advise Todd.

questions.description: On 6 January 2023, Ross enters into a contract with Marie, who runs a… Company.
questions.text: Ross runs a transport company. On 6 January 2023, Ross enters into a contract with Marie, who runs a chain of supermarkets across Ireland. The contract provides that Ross will transport frozen goods from Dublin Port to Marie's supermarkets once a month from February 2023 to February 2024. Payment is to be monthly, with the first payment of €500 due on 28" February 2023. However, in mid-January Ross does a review of his business. He calculates that he will be performing this contract at a loss, and he would be better off entering a transport agreement with someone else at a higher rate per month. He calls Marie to cancel the agreement. Marie is furious, as she says they had a binding contract and it will now be more expensive to hire another transporter. Ross thinks he can escape the deal as Marie has not yet paid any money or any deposit. Ross also has an ongoing arrangement with a kitchen unit manufacturer, Kitchens Ltd, to transport kitchen units and kitchen equipment to different locations around the country. Whenever Kitchen Ltd needs something transported, the manager Carla simply phones Ross with the details told Ross organises the transport. This has happened approximately twice a month for the past two years, although there is no written contract in place. However, Carla has recently told Ross that she is not going to continue placing orders with him. Ross believes that an implied contract exists because of their ongoing relationship, and that Carla cannot simply cancel this contract. He believes that this implied contract includes an implied duty of good faith that requires Kitchens Ltd/Carla to give reasonable notice of six months to terminate the contract. Advise Ross.

questions.description: Niamh is an internationally recognised collector of vintage books and… Advise Niamh.
questions.text: Niamh is an internationally recognised collector of vintage books and manuscripts. She attends a book fair where she meets Sean, who is selling a selection of books and notebooks, Sean explains to her that his grandfather was the famous author Peter McClavey, and he is selling a collection of books and notebooks that'he found in his grandfather's attic. Niamh inspects the books at Sean's stand and is delighted to find a set of notebooks with what looks like handwritten notes relating to Peter McClavey's most famsus book,, Times of our Lives. Niamh exclaims that the notes would be "great for research!" and Sean says he can sell them to her for €5,000. However, Sean does not admit that although the notebooks came from his grandfather's attic, they were not in fact those of the author Peter McClavey, but rather were written by his brother, Paul McClavey after the publication of Times of our Lives. When Niamh tells Sean sympathetically that it must be hard to part with his grandfather's work, Sean responds merely by saying "No, it is not". Niamh buys notebooks for €5,000. Six months later when Niamh tries to sell the notebooks to a university, she discovers that they were not in fact written by Peter McClavey. She claims that either the contract with Sean is invalid as a result of this mistake and that she is entitled to her money back, or that Sean is liable for failing to disclose the fact that the notebooks were not in fact written by his grandfather. Advise Niamh.

questions.description: Sam, Tom and Bob are friends who meet every Thursday night for a few… Advise Sam.
questions.text: Sam, Tom and Bob are friends who meet every Thursday night for a few drinks in their local pub. One week, after four pints of beer each, Sam says that he saw that the National Lottery Jackpot will be €5 million the following Saturday, and asks if the others would be interested in "going in together" to buy multiple tickets to increase the chances of winning. Sam suggests that if they each contribute €40 they will be able to buy €120 worth of tickets. Tom immediately agrees and gives Sam €40 to buy tickets. Bob says he doesn't have cash on him, but that he will transfer money ever to Sam "as soon as he has a chance". Sam goes to the shop the next day and buys €20 worth of Lottery tickets. Bob forgets to transfer the money over to Sam, and only has a vague recollection of the entire conversation. It turns out that one of the tickets Sam bought has the winning numbers. However, Sam now wants to keep the full €5 million, and doesn't want to pay out money to either Tom or Bob. He says it wasn't a serious or legally enforceable agreement, as it was made between friends after a few drinks. In addition, he says that Bob didn't even pay his share of the money. Tom and Bob are threatening to bring legal action for their share of the winnings. Advise Sam.

questions.description: "It is possible to vary an existing contractual relationship in the absence of fresh consideration, on the basis of…
questions.text: "It is possible to vary an existing contractual relationship in the absence of fresh consideration, on the basis of promissory estoppel. However strict limitations apply." Discuss this statement with reference to case law. |In your view, is the current law satisfactory or should it be easier - or more difficult - to modify an existing contract?

questions.description: Carol has a landscape gardening business, specialising in designing,… Company.
questions.text: Carol has a landscape gardening business, specialising in designing, building, and maintaining gardens and outdoor areas for businesses such as hotels. In June, Carol designs and builds an outdoor area for a local businessman, Pat, for an agreed price of €10,000. Pat is required to pay Carol by July 15th. However, by the end of July Pat has still not paid Carol for her services. Carol presses for payment, to no avail. Eventually, in August Pat tells Carol that in the "current economic climate" he is not sure he will be able to pay her in full, but he offers her €6,000 in settlement of the debt. He tells her that if this offer is not accepted then nothing will be paid. Carol is worried about her cash flow, and reluctantly accepts Pat's offer. Pat pays Carol €6,000. A month later Carol changes her mind and looks for the balance, €4,000, from Pat. However, Pat says they had a binding agreement that she would accept €6,000 and she cannot now claim the €4,000. In addition, since 2016 Carol has regularly done landscaping work for a local hotel. They have no written contract, but approximately twice a month the hotel asks Carol to come in to maintain the gardens and outdoor areas. However, Carol recently learned that the hotel plans to hire a large company, Landscapes Ltd, to do this work at a cheaper rate, and that the hotel will not be asking her to come in anymore. Carol wants to know if the hotel can simply terminate their "arrangement" without notice. She thinks she should have received at least 6 months' notice of any changes given the long-term, albeit informal, relationship between them. Advise Carol.

questions.description: Tom is a buyer for a manufacturer of tomato sauces. Frustrat, Breach Of Contract.
questions.text: Tom is a buyer for a manufacturer of tomato sauces. In April and May 2023, he was in negotiations with Sarah, a producer of tomatoes based in Ireland, about the purchase of her entire tomato crop for the next 3 years. During a phone call in May, they agreed to a price of €2 per kilogram of tomatoes, with payment to be made on a monthly basis. During their discussion Sarah brought up the issue of the delivery schedule, and Tom said "ah we can figure that sort of detail out as we go along". Tom followed up this phone call with a text message to Sarah, in which he said: "Great to talk today! Am happy with the €2 per kilo price tag. I'll get our team here to put together the formal bit but it's great that you're on board." Sarah replies to the message with a "smiley face" emoji, as she is busy when she receives the text. However, the next day Sarah contacts Tom to say that she is not going to go ahead with the sale. She says that there was never a fully concluded agreement as they never agreed on the delivery schedule, and it is only an oral agreement which is not enforceable. Tom wishes to enforce the contract. In January 2022, Tom also entered into a 5 year contract with Carlos, a tomato producer in Spain. However, in the summer of 2023 the extreme weather conditions in Spain have meant that Carlos has been unable to supply the agreed quantity of tomatoes. Tom has had to buy tomatoes from another source in Northern Europe, at a more expensive contract rate, and he wants to bring an action against Carlos for breach of contract to recover the difference in price. Carlos is arguing that the contract has been frustrated by the extreme weather conditions, which have meant that the tomato crop has been reduced by 50% this year. The contract is governed by Irish law but there is no mention of what will happen if Carlos cannot supply the full amount of tomatoes due to adverse conditions. Advise Tom.

questions.description: During the summer of 2023, Mark decided to take up hiking. Company.
questions.text: During the summer of 2023, Mark decided to take up hiking. On 5th July 2023 he went to his local outdoor sports shop, Hike Ltd, and bought a raincoat described on the label as "Waterproof even under extreme weather conditions". He bought the raincoat in the sales, at a reduced price. However, when he wore the raincoat out on a hike the next weekend there was heavy rain and the coat leaked, meaning he had to abandon his hike early. When he brought the raincoat back to the shop on 15th July, the shop assistant pointed to a sign in front of the till that said "Exchange only on sale items" and said that he is not entitled to a full refund. By the end of July, poor weather means that Mark has given up on hiking, and he goes back to his favourite hobby which is playing online video games. He buys a new online game, Buzzcraft, from a software company Vendo Ltd, but from the very beginning he finds that the software glitches and won't save any of his changes or moves. When he contacts Vendo Ltd they send him a link to upload software to fix the problem, but they tell Mark that there is a €10 fee for this "upgrade". Mark wants to know if he has to pay this fee. Advise Mark on his legal rights under the Consumer Rights Act 2022, which would apply to these contracts. Briefly comment on improvements, if any, the Act has made to Mark's legal position as a consumer in these two situations.

questions.description: Ben grows hops which he sells to various independent breweries. Breach Of Contract, Conditions Of Sale.
questions.text: Ben grows hops which he sells to various independent breweries. Celine approaches Ben with a view to buying a quantity of hops to make her very popular beer. Celine asks if the hops have been treated with sulphur, and says that she will not even discuss the price if they have. Ben assures her that they have not. Celine is delighted and they discuss the price and delivery details. Ben then presents her with a document which he describes as "my conditions of sale". A clause in the document states as follows: "In the event of any hops sold not complying with the express terms of the contract of sale, we will refund all payments made to us by the buyer and this shall be the limit of our obligation. We hereby exclude all liability for any loss or damage arising from the use of any hops supplied by us and for any consequential loss or damage arising out of such use." Celine agrees to buy the hops and signs the document Ben has given her, but does not read through the terms. Later, after she has made the beer, Celine discovers that sulphur was used on a small portion of the hops. As a result, Celine has to dispose of her entire production of beer, suffering significant loss. When she announces her intention to sue Ben for breach of contract, he argues that the contract she signed does not mention sulphur, and that his statement about the sulphur was a mere representation and not a term of the contract. He also claims that whether it was a representation or a term, the limitation clause covers the event which has occurred and that Celine is merely entitled to a refund. Advise Celine.

questions.description: Jane is the manager of a leisure centre which is undergoing some… Breach Of Contract, Damages.
questions.text: Jane is the manager of a leisure centre which is undergoing some renovations. She enters into a contract with Tony, whereby Tony agrees to supply and install a jacuzzi five metres long and one metre deep in the centre, at a cost of €5,000. However, when the jacuzzi is installed, Jane is annoyed because it is only four and a half metres long and ninety centimetres deep. Other than the difference in size, the jacuzzi is perfectly useable and has all the features Jane requires. Jane wants damages of €10,000 to cover the cost of tearing out the jacuzzi that has been installed and replacing it with a properly installed, bigger jacuzzi. She wants further damages for the stress and inconvenience of getting the jacuzzi replaced. Tony admits that he is in breach of contract, but he is refusing to pay damages. He says that the difference in the size of the jacuzzi is only minor, and that the jacuzzi installed is still worth at least €4,500. Jane also orders a specialised industrial floor cleaner from Supplies Ltd, to keep the floors of the swimming pool and changing rooms clean and dry. The floor cleaner is due to arrive in February 2023; however, it is only delivered in June 2023. For this time period Jane had to hire extra people to keep the floors clean and dry, as she did not have any other machine to use. She wants to sue Supplies Ltd for the losses caused as a result of the delay in delivery, but Supplies Ltd says they couldn't possibly have known that she had no other machine and that she would have to hire people to do the work manually. Advise Jane.

questions.description: Elliot is the owner of a recording studio. Breach Of Contract.
questions.text: Elliot is the owner of a recording studio. He rents out his recording studio to musicians for a fee of €300 per day. In July 2023, he enters into a contract to rent out the studio to Fiona for the month of September, with the full fee for the month payable on 1st September. At the start of August, however, Fiona contacts Elliot to say that she will be using a different studio instead.When Elliot objects, Fiona says that she can do this because she didn't have to pay a deposit and she hadn't paid any money yet. Elliot wants advice on whether he can still insist that Fiona pay the full amount owing when it falls due and on other options that are open to him. Elliot also occasionally acts as an agent for up-and-coming musicians and singers. Three years ago, he agreed to act as an agent for Hannah, a 15 year old singer at the start of her career. The contract stated that in return for representing Hannah, Elliot was to receive a commission of 25% of all Hannah's income, from music or related merchandise, for the next 10 years. However, now that Hannah is 18 she has decided that she is unhappy with the arrangement with Elliot, and she has told Elliot that she wants to cancel their contract and look for a better deal with another agent. Elliot wants to sue Hannah for breach of contract. Advise Elliot.

questions.description: Coldstore Ltd is a transporter of refrigerated pharmaceuticals to… Penalty Clause, Company.
questions.text: Coldstore Ltd is a transporter of refrigerated pharmaceuticals to businesses across Ireland, with its own fleet of refrigerated trucks to make deliveries. In January 2023 Coldstore Ltd entered into a 2 year transport and delivery contract with Merith Ltd. Term 6 of the contract stated that Coldstore Ltd would collect products from Merith Ltd every Monday and deliver them within 48 hours to specified locations across Ireland. In June 2023, however, Coldstore Ltd experienced staffing difficulties and had a shortage of drivers. For 3 consecutive weeks they collected products from Merith on Tuesday instead of Monday, and these goods were not delivered within the 48-hour window. Although Coldstore Ltd was willing to pay compensation for any losses caused as a result, Merith Ltd is unhappy with Coldstore Ltd's performance and wishes to terminate the contract as a result of these breaches of contract. Coldstore Ltd says that these were "unfortunate isolated incidents' caused by a situation which is now resolved, and it thinks that that the real reason Merith Ltd wants to terminate is to take advantage of a cheaper contract price elsewhere. Coldstore Ltd wants to know if Merith Ltd has any grounds to terminate the contract. In May 2023, Coldstore Ltd agreed to be a transporter for a large pharmaceutical company, Xyntath Ltd, with the manager Tom signing the pharmaceutical company's standard form contract. In June 2023, one of the deliveries was two days late due to the shortage of drivers. Xyntath Ltd is seeking to rely on the following clause of its contract with Coldstore Ltd: "In the event that any delivery is more than 24 hours late, the transporter shall be responsible for the costs of all and any delays in production and a payment of 150% of the cost of the material transported shall be immediately due." Coldstore Ltd believes that this is a penalty clause and should not be enforceable. Advise Coldstore Ltd.

questions.description: Which contractual principles are used by the judiciary to protect persons who provide guarantees and securities to…
questions.text: Which contractual principles are used by the judiciary to protect persons who provide guarantees and securities to financial institutions that advance monies to close friends and family members of the guarantor? Refer to cases in your answer.
